mnemonics.title: TYPES OF USER INTERFACES
mnemonics.content: CGBTV: COMMAND-LINE, GRAPHICAL, BATCH, TOUCH, VOICE - FIVE WAYS TO TALK TO YOUR MACHINE

mnemonics.title: WIMP IN GUI
mnemonics.content: WINDOWS ICONS MENUS POINTER - VISUAL ELEMENTS THAT MAKE COMPUTERS USER-FRIENDLY

mnemonics.title: SHELL FUNCTIONS
mnemonics.content: READ PARSE EXECUTE - THE THREE STEPS A SHELL TAKES TO RUN YOUR COMMAND

mnemonics.title: SYSTEM CALL TRANSITION
mnemonics.content: USER TO KERNEL MODE - WHERE PRIVILEGED OPERATIONS HAPPEN SAFELY

acronyms.usage: Text-based interface where users type commands like 'ls' or 'dir' to interact with the OS
Visual interface using windows, icons, menus, and pointers for user interaction
The four core elements that define a graphical user interface
Text-based interface using ASCII characters and keyboard navigation (e.g., BIOS setup)
Interface that accepts voice commands like Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant
Set of functions/calls that allow applications to request services from the OS
Early OS that used CLI for all user interactions

visualTips.concept: User Mode vs Kernel Mode Transition
visualTips.visualization: Picture a security checkpoint: user programs (citizens) need a guard (system call) to enter the restricted kernel area (government building). Without proper authorization (system call), they cannot access privileged operations.
Think of a building: USER APPS are on top floor, SHELL is the reception desk, SYSTEM CALLS are the elevator, KERNEL is the basement where all the real work happens.
CLI is like speaking directly (fast, efficient), GUI is like having a translator with pictures (slower but easier). Experts use CLI for speed, beginners use GUI for simplicity.

Imagine a border crossing: user programs live in 'User Country' with limited resources, system calls are the border gates that temporarily allow entry to 'Kernel Country' where all the powerful operations happen.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant waiter: takes your order (commands), communicates it to the kitchen (OS kernel), and brings back the food (results/output) to you.
Like a bank withdrawal form: you (user program) fill it out with details, the bank (OS) processes it in the secure area (kernel mode), and you get your money (service).
Like a car's dashboard with buttons and gauges: visual, intuitive, easy to understand but may not give access to all underlying controls.
Like a pilot's control panel: lots of switches and knobs, requires training to use, but gives direct and precise control over all systems.
Like a TV remote control: it's the middleman between you (the viewer) and the TV (computer), translating your button presses into actions on the device.
Like a drive-through restaurant at off-peak hours: you place your order, it's prepared and ready when you arrive - no waiting for cooking during the actual visit.
